topic: mixing help — muddy low end at 200 Hz

turns.content: ok I need help with a mix!! I've been working on this track all night and the low end is driving me CRAZY. something around 200Hz is just MUD. I can't get it to sit right no matter what I do!!

200Hz muddiness is one of the most common mixing problems -- you're definitely not alone. Before we dive in, can you tell me what's going on in that frequency range? What instruments are fighting for space down there -- bass, kick, synths?

so I've got a sub bass, the kick obviously, and there's a synth pad that's got a lot of low frequency content too. when they all hit together it just turns into this woolly mess. the riddim loses all its punch!!

That's your problem right there -- three elements all competing in the same frequency neighborhood. Here's how I'd approach it:

1. **High-pass filter the synth pad** -- roll off everything below 200-250Hz on that pad. It doesn't need to live down there, and it's just crowding out the bass and kick.

2. **Separate the kick and bass** -- if your kick's fundamental is around 60-80Hz, scoop a little out of the bass at that same frequency to give the kick room. And vice versa -- find where the bass's fundamental lives and cut the kick there slightly.

3. **Notch around 200Hz specifically** -- try a narrow cut (Q of about 3-4) on whatever sounds worst at 200Hz. Sometimes 2-3dB is all you need.

I high passed the pad already at like 150 but maybe that's not enough?? and yeah the kick and bass are definitely fighting. the kick has a lot of body around 100-200 that I don't want to lose tho because it gives it that CHEST feel you know??

Bump that high-pass on the pad up to at least 250Hz, maybe even 300Hz. You'll be surprised how much you can cut without losing the pad's character -- most of what makes a pad sound good is in the mids and highs anyway.

For the kick body, I totally understand wanting to keep that chest-punch feel. Instead of cutting the kick, try this:

- **Sidechain compress the bass to the kick.** Every time the kick hits, the bass ducks out of the way for just a moment. Set a fast attack (1-5ms) and a medium release (50-100ms). This lets them share the same space but take turns.
- A 3-4dB reduction on the bass when the kick hits is usually enough. You'll feel the kick punch through without losing the bass energy.
sidechain!! ok I've been doing that but maybe my release is too long because it feels like the bass is pumping too much. like you can HEAR the compression happening and it sounds unnatural

If you can hear the pumping, your release is definitely too long -- or your ratio/threshold is too aggressive. Try this:

- **Shorten the release to around 40-60ms** -- just enough for the kick transient to punch through, then the bass comes right back.
- **Lower the ratio** -- if you're at 4:1 or higher, try 2:1 or 3:1. You want subtle ducking, not obvious pumping.
- **Raise the threshold** so it's only compressing by 2-3dB max.

Another option: instead of sidechain compression, try **sidechain EQ** or "dynamic EQ." You can set a band around 100-200Hz on the bass that only ducks when the kick hits. It's more transparent than full-range compression because you're only affecting the frequencies that actually clash.
DYNAMIC EQ!! I've heard about that but never actually tried it. so it's like an EQ cut that only kicks in when the kick drum hits?? that sounds way cleaner than what I'm doing now

Exactly! It's an EQ band that reacts dynamically -- it sits there doing nothing until the sidechain signal (your kick) triggers it, and then it cuts only the specific frequencies that are clashing, only for the duration of the kick hit. 

To set it up:
1. Put a dynamic EQ on your bass track.
2. Create a band centered around 150-200Hz (wherever the kick and bass are clashing most).
3. Set that band to be triggered by a sidechain input from your kick.
4. Dial in a 3-5dB cut with a fast attack and release.

The result is much more transparent than regular sidechain compression because you're surgically removing just the problem frequencies instead of ducking the entire bass signal. The rest of the bass spectrum stays untouched.

ok I'm about to try this right now!! this might be the ting that saves this track still. I've been going back and forth on this mix for like 4 hours and my ears are cooked lol. one more question tho — should I be mixing on headphones or monitors for low end stuff??

For low end work, **monitors in a treated room** are always better because you physically feel the bass and the room gives you a more natural stereo image. Headphones tend to exaggerate low end separation and can fool you.

But if your room isn't well treated (which is most home studios), headphones can actually be more reliable because you're taking the room out of the equation. 

Best approach: **check on both.** Mix on monitors first, then put on headphones to verify. If the low end sounds good on both, you're in a good spot. And after 4 hours, your ears are definitely fatigued -- take a break, come back tomorrow with fresh ears, and I bet things will click faster. Low end decisions on tired ears usually lead to overcorrecting.

yeah you're right I need to step away!! I always do this — I get locked in and don't stop until the sun comes up lol. ok I'm gonna try the dynamic EQ ting tomorrow with fresh ears. THANK YOU!! this was actually super helpful!!

Ha, classic producer move -- losing track of time in the studio. Get some rest and hit it fresh tomorrow. The dynamic EQ should make a big difference, and bumping up that high-pass on the pad too. Good luck with the track!
